findings.message: Using `try_exists()` as the presence check here misclassifies dangling symlinks as "absent". A broken `spec.json` or catalogue can therefore take the skip path / `Ok(RefVerifyScope::All)` instead of the intended fail-closed error, so the IN-07 contract can be bypassed. The same pattern appears in `pair_source::enumerate_chain1_pairs` at `pair_source.rs:65-70`.

`resolve()` still reads and parses `architecture-rules.json` via `load_bindings()` before it can return `All`. That violates the new existence-only contract in the briefing and makes the Phase 0 success path depend on unrelated rules-file corruption or I/O, which the resolver is supposed to avoid.

P1: `RefVerifyScopeResolver::load_bindings()` now does a plain `read_to_string()` after only a leaf-level presence check, instead of using the guarded `load_tddd_layers()` path. That drops the descriptor-pinned/symlink-rejecting open that previously protected `architecture-rules.json`, so a symlinked rules path or symlinked parent can now be followed and parsed from outside the repo root.

P1: the new `spec.json`-absent fast path treats any `NotFound` from `symlink_metadata()` as a benign phase-0 state and returns `Ok(Vec::new())` before any guarded directory/file validation. If `track/items/<id>` itself is missing or otherwise invalid, `load_pairs()` now silently succeeds with zero pairs, which can let `run` / `check-approved` pass on a nonexistent track instead of failing closed as the previous `read_guarded_text()` path did.

`path_is_present` only lstat's the leaf path. A symlinked `track/items` or `track_id` parent can therefore make `resolve()` classify files outside `project_root`, and the new `pair_source` skip path depends on the same check. This breaks the fail-closed boundary for scope resolution.

The new `track_dir.symlink_metadata()` / `spec_path.symlink_metadata()` checks still follow symlinked parent components. A symlinked track tree outside the repo can hit the `[SKIP]` branch and return `Ok(vec![])` instead of failing closed, bypassing the guarded-read path.

`resolve()` is no longer existence-only: after checking `spec.json`, it calls `load_bindings()` and parses `architecture-rules.json` before deciding the scope. That means a malformed rules file now fails the resolver itself, even though the briefing says broken-but-present artifacts should be handled downstream and the resolver should rely on path existence only. In the phase0/spec-only happy path this turns an otherwise `Ok(All)` run into an `Io` error.

Treating a missing `spec.json` as `Ok(Vec::new())` is only safe for the pure Phase-0 case. In `All` scope, this bypasses the old fail-closed path and lets `enumerate_chain2_all_layers` continue, so a track with catalogues but no spec can now produce Chain-2 pairs instead of erroring. That silently skips Chain-1 verification whenever `load_pairs` is called directly or the resolver is bypassed.

The resolver is no longer existence-only: it parses `architecture-rules.json`, and `load_bindings` converts `LoadTdddLayersError::Parse(_)` into an empty binding set. That makes a malformed rules file indistinguishable from 'no TDDD layers declared' and can return `Ok(All)` instead of failing closed at scope-resolution time, which is an architecture regression against the brief.

`resolve()` now returns `Ok(RefVerifyScope::All)` immediately when `architecture-rules.json` is missing, and the only Phase-0 catalogue check when the file exists is the `*-types.json` directory scan. That means a spec-less track can still pass even if catalogue files are already present but the rules file is absent, or if the declared `tddd.catalogue_file` uses a valid custom name that does not match the suffix heuristic. Both cases violate the fail-closed IN-06/IN-05 contract.

`resolve()` returns `Ok(All)` as soon as `architecture-rules.json` is missing, even if `track/items/<track_id>` already contains a `*-types.json` catalogue. That is a fail-open for the IN-06 case: a catalogue-present/spec-absent track will be treated as Phase 0 and allowed to proceed into `All` scope instead of failing closed.

`resolve()` still parses `architecture-rules.json` via `load_bindings()` to decide scope. That makes scope resolution depend on rules-file contents, so a malformed rules file can reject otherwise valid phase-0/spec-only tracks. The brief says the resolver should do existence checks only and leave broken-file handling to pair_source/codec.

`resolve()` only treats files ending in `-types.json` as catalogues when deciding whether to fail closed for IN-06. `architecture-rules.json` permits arbitrary safe `catalogue_file` names, so a track that uses a valid nonstandard catalogue filename will be misclassified as having no catalogues and can resolve to `All` even when `spec.json` is absent. That bypasses the intended ordering check and can let a catalogue-present track proceed as if it were Phase 0.

`reject_symlinks_below(track_dir, project_root)` only proves the path exists and is not a symlink; it does not require `track_dir` to be a directory. If `track/items/<track_id>` is an existing regular file, this branch succeeds, `spec.json` is then treated as absent, and the function returns `Ok(Vec::new())` instead of erroring. That lets an invalid track root masquerade as the Phase 0 zero-pair case. Use a directory guard here (`open_guarded_directory` / `is_dir()` check) before taking the skip path.

The Chain-2 defensive presence pass still probes catalogues with `Path::try_exists` and later `exists`, which follow symlinks and report dangling symlinks as absent. If an All-scope path reaches this helper with dangling catalogue symlinks, it can take the all-absent branch and return zero Chain-2 pairs instead of fail-closing, violating the symlink-hardening contract described in the briefing. Use the same symlink-aware presence check as the resolver for catalogue probes and missing-list construction.

`test_ref_verify_run_phase0_no_artifacts_exits_zero` is not hermetic: it does not put a fake `claude` binary on `PATH`, so if the zero-pair assumption regresses the test can escape to a host-installed verifier instead of failing deterministically. Keep the external verifier mocked even for the phase-0 case.

`test_ref_verify_check_approved_phase0_no_artifacts_exits_zero` only checks `exit_code == 0`; it does not assert the user-visible success message that identifies the intended `No production reference pairs found` path. That leaves a coverage gap where a different success path could regress without this test failing.

`task-coverage.json` maps `AC-02` only to `T001`, but the external-surface change that actually removes the hardcoded `--context commit-gate` invocation lives in `T004` (`impl-plan.json` lines 23-25). As written, the coverage matrix says the Phase 0 commit-gate acceptance is satisfied without the task that updates the commit-gate call site, so the coverage for the main gate path is incomplete.

The new ADR file starts at the title and context without the required `## Status` section defined by the ADR template in `knowledge/adr/README.md` (`## ADR テンプレート`, lines 21-29). That leaves the ADR's lifecycle state implicit instead of recorded in the document itself, which is inconsistent with the repository's ADR contract.

CN-03 says only the fully absent case is skippable and that every other combination must fail, but AC-06 and T001 explicitly require the spec-present / all-catalogues-absent case to exit 0 as Chain2 zero pairs. This makes the constraint inconsistent with the acceptance criteria and the implementation plan.

`D9` is now marked `superseded` in frontmatter, but the prose still says only the calling-context scope resolution part is replaced and that the dedicated command surface / diff cache remain valid. That leaves the same decision simultaneously retired and still active, which is an internal contradiction; either keep `D9` active with a partial-supersession note or split the surviving parts into a separate decision.

T004 omits `track-active-gate`, but IN-04 and AC-12 require both `track-commit-message` and `track-active-gate` to be updated. As written, the plan only covers one required Makefile surface, so AC-12 is not fully achievable.

T001's success path always returns `RefVerifyScope::All`; it never produces `Chain1` or `Chain2`. That means the planned resolver does not actually resolve scope from artifact existence and cannot encode the chain-specific skip rules required by IN-01 / CN-03 / AC-01 / AC-06 / AC-07.

AC-12 says the Makefile's `track-commit-message / track-active-gate` ref-verify calls are updated, but T004 explicitly says `track-active-gate` has no ref-verify invocation and needs no script change. That makes the acceptance criterion ambiguous about whether `track-active-gate` is actually in scope.

T001’s resolver logic returns `Ok(RefVerifyScope::All)` for every non-error case, so the `RefVerifyScope` variants `Chain1`/`Chain2` are never selected. That conflicts with the usecase contract that `RefVerifyScopeResolver` derives the active scope from artifact existence and returns that resolved scope to cli-composition.

T001 changes `RefVerifyScopeResolver::resolve` to the new `resolve(&str)` signature before any caller is updated. The dependent callsites are only rewritten in T002, so a commit after T001 leaves `apps/cli-composition` uncompilable. As written, the task split cannot be executed as a small-commit sequence.

T002 removes `context` / `layer` from `RefVerifyRunInput`, but `apps/cli/src/commands/ref_verify.rs` is only updated in T003. That creates another required intermediate state that does not compile, so the T002→T003 ordering is not physically executable as a standalone task boundary.

T001 says malformed `spec.json` is an `Io` error in `RefVerifyScopeResolver::resolve`, but the later pair_source step says the same malformed `spec.json` should still surface as `RefVerifyError::VerifierPort`. The plan never reconciles which layer owns JSON parsing or which error is user-visible, so the failure mode is internally inconsistent.

The boundary note claims that once `resolver` returns `Ok(All)`, catalogue files must be completely absent. That is false in the same task description, which also returns `Ok(All)` for the spec-present/all-catalogue-present and spec-present/all-catalogue-absent cases. This would mislead the Chain① skip-path implementation.

`RefVerifyScopeResolver` is documented as failing on parse failures here, but the implementation plan keeps parse-不能 artifacts in `pair_source`/codec and surfaces them as `RefVerifyError::VerifierPort` instead. That splits IN-07 across two layers and makes the planned error contract inconsistent.
